elements.sent-2411-003: Video understanding has made huge strides in recent years, relying largely on the power of the transformer architecture.
elements.sent-2411-004: As this architecture is notoriously expensive and video is highly redundant, research into improving efficiency has become particularly relevant.
elements.sent-2411-005: This has led to many creative solutions, including token merging and token selection.
elements.sent-2411-006: While most methods succeed in reducing the cost of the model and maintaining accuracy, an interesting pattern arises: most methods do not outperform the random sampling baseline.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this paper we take a closer look at this phenomenon and make several observations.
elements.sent-2411-008: First, we develop an oracle for the value of tokens which exposes a clear Pareto distribution where most tokens have remarkably low value, and just a few carry most of the perceptual information.
elements.sent-2411-009: Second, we analyze why this oracle is extremely hard to learn, as it does not consistently coincide with visual cues.
elements.sent-2411-010: Third, we observe that easy videos need fewer tokens to maintain accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-011: We build on these and further insights to propose a lightweight video model we call \model that can select a small number of tokens effectively, outperforming state-of-the-art and existing baselines across datasets (Kinetics400 and Something-Something-V2) in the challenging trade-off of computation (GFLOPs) vs accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-012: Video understanding has made remarkable progress in the last few years, getting close to solving many standard benchmarks and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-013: This progress has largely hinged on the use of the transformer architecture~\cite{vaswani2017attention , which is both extremely powerful as well as extremely computationally expensive.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Transformers originated in the language field, where few tokens are needed to represent a concept, for example an action, as tokens roughly correspond to single words.
elements.sent-2411-015: This is not the case in the visual adaptation of transformers~\cite{Arnab2021ViViTAV , where the number of tokens to represent an action is orders of magnitude larger, leading to an exponentially larger computational cost.}
elements.sent-2411-016: This cost has a wide range of negative consequences: it limits the deployability of models, as they require expensive equipment to run, it burdens video understanding research, making turnaround slower, it is financially expensive and it has a big environmental footprint~\cite{gowda2023watt .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In particular, although the inference cost is small compared to training, over the lifetime of a model, the overall cost of inference is larger than the cost of training, as it is done many more times~\cite{inferencecost .}
elements.sent-2411-018: At the same time, video is notoriously redundant both in space (where background regions might be a large part of the scene) and time (with many frames being similar, even if they are downsampled from the original video.
elements.sent-2411-019: These two factors together pose a great opportunity opening a research theme on token reduction.
elements.sent-2411-020: This theme has led to many creative ideas on how to reduce the amount of tokens.
elements.sent-2411-021: One of the noticeable strands is token-merging, where similar tokens are grouped together such that only a representative of the group needs to be stored in memory \cite{tome,Ding2023PruneST,choi2024vid,Feng2023EfficientVT .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Other ideas have revolved around selecting tokens based on motion, such as the Motionformer~\cite{Patrick2021KeepingYE .}
elements.sent-2411-023: These models can reduce the computational cost in \gflops considerably, while keeping accuracy on-par with the full model.
elements.sent-2411-024: Crucially, we observe that most of these models make a trade-off between accuracy and computation that is similar or inferior to the simplest baseline of sampling tokens randomly (see Fig.~\ref{fig:random-strong ).}
elements.sent-2411-025: In this paper we take a closer look at this puzzling effect, and discover some underlying principles of the nature of visual tokens that are useful for designing faster, more efficient video models.

The ``P-Ratio'' represents the proportion of tokens selected relative to the total number of tokens per video. } \label{fig:random-strong}
elements.sent-2411-026: First, we design an oracle that estimates the value of each token for a particular task, concretely we take {\em Action Classification as our testbed.}
elements.sent-2411-027: This oracle is created such that the value of each input token corresponds to its gradient~\cite{Selvaraju2016GradCAMVE .}
elements.sent-2411-028: We refer to it as an oracle because it uses the ground truth label of the class.
elements.sent-2411-029: Given this oracle, we can now sample a subset of the tokens according to their gradient value, keeping those with higher values.
elements.sent-2411-030: We observe that the accuracy of using a subset of tokens of a video according to the oracle is actually much larger than the baseline of using all tokens.
elements.sent-2411-031: In other words, ``low-value" tokens not only do not help, but actually act as noise that hurt classification.
elements.sent-2411-032: Crucially, this gap is also shockingly large: it can be up to 9\%.
elements.sent-2411-033: This demonstrates even further the surprising value of the oracle.

elements.section-2411-003: Principles of Visual Tokens in Video
elements.sent-2411-068: In this section we take a closer look at the nature of visual tokens in video.
elements.sent-2411-069: The goal is to use the underlying principles to design an efficient model that can both reduce the computational complexity of models at test time as well as leverage the overwhelming redundancy of tokens in video.
elements.sent-2411-070: We introduce the experimental setup, including architectures, datasets, and implementation details used throughout the paper, followed by key principles and supporting experimental evidence.. \myparagraph{Datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-071: We use two standard action recognition datasets: Kinetics-400 \cite{carreira2017quo , sourced from YouTube, is a large-scale human action video dataset with 400 classes.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Each class has a minimum of 400 action clips.
elements.sent-2411-073: Something-Something V2 (SS-V2) \cite{Goyal2017TheS focuses on fine-grained understanding of human hand gestures.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Comprising 220,847 labeled video clips, it captures 174 individuals performing predefined actions with everyday items.
elements.sent-2411-075: This dataset poses a greater challenge for temporal modeling \cite{SevillaLara2019OnlyTC .. \myparagraph{Implementation Details.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: We use VideoMAE \cite{Tong2022VideoMAEMA as backbone as it is very widely used.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Frames in each video are uniformly sampled and clips of size \(16 \times 224 \times 224\) pixels are used as network inputs with a tube size of \(2 \times 16 \times 16\).
elements.sent-2411-078: During inference, we use two temporal clips.
elements.sent-2411-079: For each temporal clip, we take three spatial crops (top-left, center, bottom-right) of size \(224 \times 224\) pixels.
elements.sent-2411-080: The final prediction is obtained by averaging the scores from all views (temporal clips \(\times\) spatial crops).
elements.sent-2411-081: All experiments were carried out using 2 NVIDIA RTX 3090 24GB GPUs.

elements.sent-2411-098: To identify which tokens play a decisive role in the classification task and to assign a score to each token based on their importance, we design an oracle to predict the value of a given token, using \gradcam~\cite{Selvaraju2016GradCAMVE .}
elements.sent-2411-099: As mentioned before, we call this an oracle because it has access to privileged information -- the true class label.
elements.sent-2411-100: However, this is indeed a tractable approximation of a ``real oracle", which would be to find the $k$-subset of tokens that minimizes the loss relative to the true label.
elements.sent-2411-101: We first calculate the gradient of the score for the target class $c$, with respect to the feature map activations of the MLP in the last blocks: \begin{equation}\omega_d^c=\frac{1}{N} \sum_t \sum_h \sum_w \ \frac{\partial y^c}{\partial A_{t h w}^d}\end{equation} where $y^c$ is the score before applying softmax for target class $c$, $A_{t h w ^d$ is the feature map activations at position $\{t,h,w\}$ with the feature $d$.}
elements.sent-2411-102: After obtaining the gradient score, we perform average pooling across all tokens to derive an importance weight $\omega_d^c$ for each feature of the target class $c$.
elements.sent-2411-103: To approximate the ground truth as closely as possible and create a more accurate oracle, the target class selected here is the true label class.
elements.sent-2411-104: After obtaining the importance weights for each feature, we calculate the importance score of each token for the target class using a linear combination.
elements.sent-2411-105: We incorporate a ReLU function when deriving the final token score to ensure that only tokens with a positive impact on the class are considered, making the activation maps clearer and concentrated: \begin{equation}S^c=ReLU\left(\sum_d \omega_d^c A^d\right)\end{equation} where $A^d$ is the feature map activations with the feature $d$, $\omega_d^c$ is the importance weight of feature $d$ at target class $c$, and $S^c\in \mathbb{R ^{t\times h \times w}$ is the final token score for the target class $c$.}

elements.sent-2411-247: Video understanding has made huge strides in recent years, relying largely on the power of transformers.
elements.sent-2411-248: As this architecture is notoriously expensive and video data is highly redundant, research into improving efficiency has become particularly relevant.
elements.sent-2411-249: Some creative solutions include token selection and merging.
elements.sent-2411-250: While most methods succeed in reducing the cost of the model and maintaining accuracy, an interesting pattern arises: most methods do not outperform the baseline of randomly discarding tokens.
elements.sent-2411-251: In this paper we take a closer look at this phenomenon and observe 5 principles of the nature of visual tokens.
elements.sent-2411-252: For example, we observe that the value of tokens follows a clear Pareto-distribution where most tokens have remarkably low value, and just a few carry most of the perceptual information.
elements.sent-2411-253: We build on these and further insights to propose a lightweight video model, LITE, that can select a small number of tokens effectively, outperforming state-of-the-art and existing baselines across datasets (Kinetics-400 and Something-Something-V2) in the challenging trade-off of computation (GFLOPs) vs accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-254: Experiments also show that LITE generalizes across datasets and even other tasks without the need for retraining.
elements.sent-2411-255: Video understanding has made remarkable progress in the last few years, getting close to solving many standard benchmarks and tasks~\cite{carreira2017quo,gu2018ava,caba2015activitynet .}
elements.sent-2411-256: Transformers originated in the language field, where few tokens are needed to represent a concept, such as an action, as tokens roughly correspond to single words.
elements.sent-2411-257: At the same time, video is notoriously redundant in space (where background regions might be a large part of the scene) and time (with many frames being similar, even if they are downsampled).
elements.sent-2411-258: These two factors together pose a great opportunity for token reduction and have led a research theme with many creative ideas.
elements.sent-2411-259: One of the noticeable strands is token merging, where similar tokens are grouped together such that only a representative of the group needs to be stored in memory \cite{tome,Ding2023PruneST,choi2024vid,Feng2023EfficientVT .}
elements.sent-2411-260: Another direction includes selecting tokens based on motion~\cite{Patrick2021KeepingYE .}
elements.sent-2411-261: While this is promising, we observe that most token-reduction models make a trade-off between accuracy and computation that is similar or inferior to the simplest baseline of sampling tokens randomly (see Fig.~\ref{fig:random-strong ).}
elements.sent-2411-262: In this paper we take a closer look at this puzzling effect, to discover underlying principles of the nature of visual tokens.
elements.sent-2411-263: We leverage these principles to design faster, more efficient video models.

elements.sent-2411-295: Given that the scores $s$ from the \oracle are in the range $[0,1]$, we incorporate a sigmoid function for better estimation: \begin{equation}\mathbf{\hat{s}}=\operatorname{Sigmoid}\left(\mathrm{MLP}\left(\mathbf{p} ; \boldsymbol{\theta}\right)\right),\end{equation} where $\mathbf{p}$ is the patch embedding, $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is the network weights, and $\mathbf{\hat{s }$ is the sequence of all estimated tokens score.}
elements.sent-2411-296: It is shown in the yellow region of Fig.~\ref{fig:overall_structure .}
elements.sent-2411-297: During inference, $\mathbf{\hat{s }$ is computed, which contains the estimated score $\mathbf{\hat{s (n,t)$ for each embedding $\mathbf{p _n^t$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-298: For a given budget $b$ given as a percentage of the total number of tokens, we select the tokens with the highest scores.
elements.sent-2411-299: This selector is inserted between the patch embedding module and the first transformer block, using all the weights of the original backbone model except those of our selector.
elements.sent-2411-300: This is similar to the case of image recognition in human perception, where we perform certain tasks faster than harder tasks \cite{humanadaptive .}

elements.sent-2411-302: We experiment extensively with three families of variants: 1) improving the oracle in different ways, 2) including global information that can allow the selector to choose tokens based on their {\em joint value, and 3) adding complexity and capacity to the selector, for example using transformer blocks or adding layers.}
elements.sent-2411-303: We report results for all these variants in the Supplementary Material and observe that none of them has an impact on the accuracy of the downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-304: This points to an interesting conclusion: the \oracle is extremely hard to predict, and the MLP achieves a balance between accuracy and avoiding overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-305: The experimental validation on \model first compares current \sota models on the classification task (Sec.~\ref{sec:main_res ), using the mainstream datasets Kinetics-400 and SS-V2 and observe that \model achieves the pareto-front of the trade-off between computation and accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-306: We then evaluate the generalization capabilities of \model on the zero-shot setting (Sec.~\ref{sec:zero-shot ) by testing a fixed selector (trained on K400) on other datasets (UCF101~\cite{soomro2012ucf101 and SS-V2) without training or fine-tuning on them.}}
elements.sent-2411-307: We observe that accuracy is barely affected, showing that important tokens are consistent across domains.
elements.sent-2411-308: We take one step further and evaluate \model on a new task (detection) and new dataset (AVA~\cite{gu2018ava ) also without training.}
elements.sent-2411-309: We again observe that mAP is barely affected while computation is reduced.
elements.sent-2411-310: We show extensive ablation experiments to understand the behavior of \model (Sec.~\ref{sec:ablation ).}
elements.sent-2411-311: Overall we observe that \model outperforms previous state-of-the-art, generalizes well to new datasets and tasks and that despite its simplicity is robust and effective.

elements.sent-2411-315: To evaluate the generalization capability of \model, we conduct two sets of experiments.
elements.sent-2411-316: First, we test LITE trained on Kinetics-400 on \ssv and UCF101 to assess its ability to generalize across different datasets within the same task.
elements.sent-2411-317: The results of Table~\ref{tab:zeroshot demonstrate LITE's strong zero-shot capability, even to \ssv which is visually very different from Kinetics-400.}
elements.sent-2411-318: Compared to our results in Table~\ref{tab:sota_ssv2 , we observe that \model exhibits strong cross-domain generalization, achieving performance close to that of a selector trained from scratch.}
elements.sent-2411-319: Second, we examine the performance of the same \model model trained on classification on an even more challenging setting: an unseen task (action detection) and unseen dataset (AVA) to evaluate its cross-task transferability.
elements.sent-2411-320: Table~\ref{tab:zeroshot highlights LITE's strong cross-task generalization ability, reducing GFLOPs by 35\% while maintaining a minimal 0.7\% drop in mAP.}
